retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrology

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: PSGN should be suspected in all children with hypertension and heart failure, even in the absence of hematuria or a history of a preceding sore throat or pyoderma.

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrogenic streptococci infection precedes PSGN, which initially affects skin or the oropharynx. More recently, PSGN is associated with skin infections (impetigo) more frequently than throat infections (pharyngitis). [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: History should be taken about the onset and duration of the disease. Symptom onset is often abrupt. In acute postinfectious GN, there is usually a latent period of up to three weeks before clinical presentation. However, the latent period varies; it is typically one to two weeks for the cases that occur after a pharyngeal infection and two to four weeks where post-dermal infection, such as pyoderma, is the cause. Nephritis occurs within one to four days of streptococcal infection usually means preexisting kidney disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) is characterized by rapid deterioration of kidney functions due to an inflammatory response (type III hypersensitivity reaction) following streptococcal infection (See Image. Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis). This condition results from specific strains of group A beta-hemolytic streptococci called nephrogenic streptococci. The disease affects the glomeruli and the small blood vessels of the kidneys. PSGN most frequently presents in children 1 to 2 weeks after a sore throat, or 6 weeks after a skin infection (impetigo). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Higher incidence of PSGN in developing countries- due to increased skin infections (pyoderma). Though the incidence in developed countries has gone down, it is still the most common cause of glomerulonephritis (GN) in children in the united states. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Nephron -- Clinical Significance -- Nephritic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: As with nephrotic syndrome, nephritic syndromes can also be primary or secondary. Some of the most common causes of nephritic syndrome are post-infectious glomerulonephritis, IgA nephropathy, and lupus nephritis. Poststreptococcal nephritic syndrome arises 2 to 3 weeks after group A B-hemolytic nephritogenic streptococcal infection of the skin (impetigo) or pharynx. It usually occurs in children but can also occur in adults. No characteristic finding on H&E stain but granular IF pattern and subepithelial immune complex deposition (“humps”) on EM. [24] IgA is the most common nephropathy worldwide. It is characterized by the IgA immune complex deposition in the mesangium of glomeruli. It commonly presents as hematuria following mucosal infections, especially gastroenteritis. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute forms of glomerulonephritis (GN) can result from either a primary renal cause or a secondary illness that causes renal manifestations. For instance, acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) is a typical example of acute glomerulonephritis secondary to a streptococcal infection; similarly, Staphylococcus aureus infection can also lead to glomerulonephritis. In recent times, however, the incidence of glomerulonephritis associated with staphylococcal has increased as opposed to the reduction of PSGN in the United States and most developed countries. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: IgA nephropathy has been found to be the most common cause of glomerulonephritis worldwide. [12] However, the incidence of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis has declined in most developed countries. As reported by Japanese researchers, the incidence of postinfectious glomerulonephritis in their country climaxed in the 1990s. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, which accounted for nearly all cases of postinfectious GN in the 1970s, has reduced to about 40-50% since the 1990s, while the percentage of Staphylococcus aureus –related nephritis rose to 30%, and hepatitis C virus-associated glomerulonephritis also increased. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Poor hygiene, overcrowding, and low socioeconomic status are important risk factors for streptococci outbreaks, and this explains the higher incidence of PSGN in impoverished countries. Genetic factors are expected to predispose to the condition since almost 40% of patients with PSGN gave a positive family history. There is no specific gene found to cause PSGN. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Proliferative Glomerulonephritis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis stems from the type and location of deposits seen on renal biopsy; the most commonly associated disease is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Class IV of lupus nephritis shows a diffuse pattern on microscopic examination. In addition, the renal involvement seen in IgA nephropathy, anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody disease, pauci immune vasculitis like granulomatosis with polyangiitis, microscopic polyangiitis, and Henoch-Schonlein purpura all show a histological pattern indicative of DPGN. Some forms of post-infectious glomerulonephritis, especially those associated with chronic infections like endocarditis, hepatitis B, and C, show a diffuse involvement of glomeruli with increased cellular proliferation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology -- Nephritic Glomerulonephritis
retrieved_docs.content: IgA nephropathy Henoch Schonlein purpura (HSP) [7] Post streptococcal glomerulonephritis. Anti-glomerular basement membrane disease Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis Granulomatosis with polyangiitis Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis Polyarteritis nodosa Idiopathic crescentic glomerulonephritis Goodpasture syndrome Lupus nephritis Hepatitis C infection [8] Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (typical presentation is with acute nephritic syndrome, however, sometimes features resembling nephrotic syndrome may occur, additionally) [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Group A Streptococcus (GAS) has been subtyped depending on the surface M protein and opacity factor, which are known to be nephrogenic and can cause PSGN. Other causes of post-infectious glomerulonephritis include bacterial infections including endocarditis, enterocolitis, pneumonia, and intraventricular shunt infections, viral (hepatitis B and C infections, human immunodeficiency virus, cytomegalovirus, Epstein Barr virus, parvovirus B19), fungal (coccidioidomycosis, histoplasmosis), and parasitic infections (malaria, leishmania, toxoplasmosis, and schistosomiasis). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically manifestations of PSGN are more common in males than in females with a ratio of 2:1. However; the incidence of subclinical PSGN is almost equal in both sexes. Racial factors were not found to play a role. The disease most frequently affects children between the age of 3 and 12 (with the peak incidence between 5 to 6 years), and seniors greater than 60 years old.

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The disease is immunological; representing a type III hypersensitivity reaction. The exact mechanism by which PSGN occurs is not fully determined. The body responds to nephrogenic streptococcal infection by forming immune complexes containing the streptococcal antigen with a human antibody. [2] Some theories suggest that these immune complexes become deposited in kidney glomeruli reaching through the circulation. Others claim that the condition results from an “in situ” formation of the antigen-antibody complex within the kidney glomeruli. This “In situ immune complex formation” is either due to a reaction against streptococci antigens deposited in the glomerular basement membrane or, according to other theories, due to an antibody reaction against glomerular components that cross-react with streptococcal antigen due to molecular mimicry. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality during the acute phase of glomerulonephritis has been estimated at around 2 and 12 percent. It has been assumed that the renal changes found in the majority of patients who survived the initial acute attack would be qualitatively similar to those noted in the fatal cases, although little direct evidence existed to support this view. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1170
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific form of glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى) that is classified as a child condition under the broader categories of nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis. It is associated with an anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي) and commonly occurs in the setting of pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية). The glomerulus is identified as the primary site of pathology for nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis, and these entities are mapped to post‑streptococcal glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي). Within the hierarchy of glomerular disease, اعتلال كبيبات الكلى also has uremia (اليوريمية) as a downstream manifestation. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis fits into the spectrum of glomerular injuries, its immunologic associations, and its clinical sequelae, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: pattern “Sudden onset AND/OR short duration.” The same rapid‑onset pattern is linked to renal disorders classified in the group “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” whose pathological site is the glomerulus. This renal disease group is a member of the drug‑reaction syndrome “متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ),” which is characterized by eosinophilia and systemic symptoms. Within the renal disease group, “Proliferative glomerulonephritis” (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري) is identified as a specific form, and the group also encompasses “post‑infectious glomerulonephritis” (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي), reflecting similar inflammatory mechanisms that can follow streptococcal or other infections. Additionally, systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ)) is included as a condition that can manifest as proliferative glomerulonephritis, linking autoimmune kidney disease to the same nephritic‑nephrotic‑sclerotic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2943
retrieved_docs.content: Nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis are identified as constituent renal manifestations of متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ), the drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms syndrome. These same renal entities are also mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي, a form of post‑infectious glomerulonephritis. This overlap indicates that kidney involvement in the drug‑induced hypersensitivity syndrome may present as nephritis, nephrosis, or renal sclerosis, and that these pathologies share clinical and pathological features with post‑streptococcal glomerular disease. Recognizing this relationship assists clinicians in monitoring renal function, diagnosing renal complications of the drug reaction, and differentiating them from other post‑infectious renal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ)) is included as a condition that can manifest as proliferative glomerulonephritis, linking autoimmune kidney disease to the same nephritic‑nephrotic‑sclerotic spectrum. Collectively, the model connects acute rheumatic heart diseases, their sudden‑onset clinical course, and a related set of glomerular renal pathologies—including proliferative, post‑infectious, and lupus‑related glomerulonephritis—that are part of a broader drug‑induced systemic syndrome, underscoring the importance of monitoring for concurrent cardiac and renal involvement in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease, a condition that manifests as renal dysfunction, and it is also a key component of pulmonary‑renal syndrome. This form of glomerular injury encompasses the entities “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” which are localized to the glomerulus and represent specific types of proliferative glomerulonephritis. These three entities are further mapped to post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, indicating that they share pathological features with glomerular inflammation that follows an infectious episode. Proliferative glomerulonephritis, the broader category that includes “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” also contains systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ) as a member, highlighting the autoimmune contribution to this pattern of kidney disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑basement membrane disease, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, and systemic lupus erythematosus are interconnected through shared glomerular pathology and renal functional impairment, underscoring the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: الدم الحاد الناتج عن فقدان الدم). Necrotizing glomerulonephritis occupies a distinct, severe position within renal pathology as a form of glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى). It is inversely related to general glomerulonephritis, linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), and can manifest as pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئية كلوية). Hierarchically, necrotizing glomerulonephritis serves as a parent condition for nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis, which together constitute the primary lesion sites of the glomerulus. Within the broader spectrum of glomerular disease, uremia is a subcategory, and proliferative glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري) represents a specific type of the nephritis‑nephrosis‑renal‑sclerosis group. Proliferative glomerulonephritis includes systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ)) and post‑infectious glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي) as member conditions,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1096
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe form of glomerular disease that falls under the broader category اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular pathology). It is associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease) and commonly occurs in the context of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). Within the disease hierarchy, necrotizing glomerulonephritis has as a child the collective entity nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis, which represents a group of related renal inflammatory and sclerotic conditions. This group is linked to متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ), indicating that drug‑induced eosinophilic systemic reactions can manifest as nephritic, nephrotic, or sclerotic renal lesions. The primary anatomic site of these lesions is the glomerulus, as indicated by the finding_site_of relationship. The collective entity nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis is also mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑streptococcal glomerulonephritis), highlighting a common etiologic pathway. Additionally, اعتلال كبيبات الكلى includes اليوريمية (uremia) as a downstream condition, reflecting the potential progression to renal failure when glomerular injury is severe. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in recognizing the spectrum of glomerular disorders, their systemic associations, and potential triggers, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 726
retrieved_docs.content: Nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis are glomerular disorders that manifest in the glomerulus and are included as members of متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ). These conditions are conceptually linked to التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري (proliferative glomerulonephritis), which is recognized as a subtype of nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis and is also a member of الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ), reflecting its role in systemic lupus erythematosus–related kidney involvement. Furthermore, nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis are mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis), indicating a correspondence between these renal pathologies and the immune‑mediated response that follows certain infections. Together, these relationships highlight how various forms of glomerular inflammation, sclerosis, and nephrotic processes intersect within drug‑induced syndromes, autoimmune disease clusters, and post‑infectious contexts, underscoring the importance of recognizing shared pathogenic pathways for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: include CKD due to hypertension, hemolytic‑uremic syndrome, and crescentic glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الهلالي). Pediatric nephrology explicitly incorporates hemolytic‑uremic syndrome and crescentic glomerulonephritis, underscoring their relevance in children. Glomerular disease (اعتلال كبيبات الكلى) encompasses acute glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد) and related entities such as necrotizing glomerulonephritis, proliferative glomerulonephritis, post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, and renal sclerosis. Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), also known as Goodpasture’s syndrome, which frequently presents as a pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية). Progressive glomerular injury can lead to uremia (اليوريمية) and end‑stage renal failure, for which unspecified dialysis (غسيل الكلى غير محدد) is recorded as a procedural concept. Hemodialysis, a specific modality of unspecified dialysis, is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: kidney performance, and glomerular disease may progress to uremia. In pediatric nephrology, hemolytic‑uremic syndrome and crescentic glomerulonephritis are key entities linked to CKD; CKD can be considered a component of hemolytic‑uremic syndrome and part of the disease spectrum of crescentic glomerulonephritis. Acute kidney inflammation and acute glomerulonephritis are synonymous and are members of acute renal failure, while proliferative glomerulonephritis includes systemic lupus erythematosus and maps to post‑streptococcal glomerulonephritis. Procedural concepts extend beyond the renal domain to include cholecystectomy (with common duct exploration), intra‑operative cholangiogram (a form of biliary imaging and bile‑duct vessel visualization), lung volume reduction surgery, and creation of arteriovenous fistulas for dialysis. These procedures are linked to imaging of biliary vessels and to vascular aneurysm categories; aortic, peripheral, and visceral artery aneurysms may lead to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 178
retrieved_docs.content: Arabic term التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري (glomerular proliferative inflammation), a specific subtype within the broader disease group, which is also mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑inflammatory glomerular inflammation), indicating a close relationship between proliferative and post‑inflammatory glomerular processes. Systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية) is listed as a member of التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري, illustrating that lupus can manifest as proliferative glomerulonephritis and highlighting how systemic autoimmune disease contributes to specific glomerular inflammatory patterns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 738
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) that is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease). This association reflects the pathogenic role of anti‑GBM antibodies, which can produce both renal injury and the متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a manifestation of اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction), necrotizing glomerulonephritis often progresses to chronic kidney impairment, clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure). The disease spectrum encompassed by the collective term nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis includes a range of inflammatory and sclerotic renal disorders. These conditions share the glomerulus as their primary site of pathology (finding_site_of glomerulus) and are mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis), highlighting a common pathway of glomerular injury following immune‑mediated inflammation. Within this broader group, متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ) (drug‑induced hypersensitivity syndrome with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) is recognized as a member, underscoring the potential for systemic drug reactions to precipitate renal inflammation and sclerosis. Overall, necrotizing glomerulonephritis and its related entities illustrate the interconnected nature of immune‑mediated renal diseases, their systemic manifestations, and the importance of recognizing both primary glomerular pathology and secondary contributors such as drug‑induced syndromes for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: The glomerulus serves as the primary anatomic site where nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis manifest. These three renal pathologies are classified as a form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري (proliferative glomerulonephritis) and are also mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis). Proliferative glomerulonephritis encompasses systemic autoimmune disease such as الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية (SMQ), indicating that systemic lupus erythematosus can present with proliferative glomerular inflammation. Recognizing the glomerular location of these conditions and their classification under both proliferative and post‑infectious glomerulonephritis aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing renal disease, particularly when systemic lupus erythematosus is involved.

Nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis form a disease cluster that maps to post‑infectious glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي) and is inversely related to proliferative glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري). This cluster’s primary finding site is the glomerulus, includes crescentic glomerulonephritis as a child condition, and is also a member of the drug‑reaction syndrome with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms. Proliferative glomerulonephritis is linked to systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية), and acute kidney inflammation (التهاب الكلية الحاد) is categorized as a member of acute renal failure (الفشل الكلوي الحاد). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 163
retrieved_docs.content: Glomerulonephritis/nefrosis corresponds to acute glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد) and serves as the parent of several renal entities: nephritis, nephrosis, nephrotic syndrome, and the grouping “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis.” Within this hierarchy, crescentic glomerulonephritis and proliferative glomerulonephritis (including systemic lupus erythematosus / الذئبة الحمامية المجموعية SMQ and post‑infectious glomerulonephritis) are defined, while necrotizing glomerulonephritis is linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and the drug‑reaction with eosinophilia syndrome. All forms of glomerulonephritis are clinically associated with chronic renal failure and uremia, and pediatric nephrology terminology adds hemolytic‑uremic syndrome as a member of the CKD SMQ. Vascular pathologies—including aortic, peripheral, and visceral artery aneurysms and aortic ulcer penetration (اختراق القرحة الأبهرية)—are recognized

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 132
retrieved_docs.content: Stomach cancer (سرطان المعدة) is clinically linked to neutropenia (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض), indicating that patients with this malignancy may develop a reduced white‑blood‑cell count. Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific form of glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) that is associated with anti‑basement‑membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), a condition that also manifests as renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). This necrotizing process frequently occurs within the context of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية), underscoring the close relationship between lung and kidney involvement in certain autoimmune disorders. The disease spectrum that includes nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis is situated at the glomerulus, the primary site of injury in these glomerular disorders.
